title: Nvidia Deepens Korea Ties as AI Infrastructure Race Intensifies
subhead: Jensen Huang's Seoul visit produced major partnerships with SK Telecom, LG, and SK hynix, while Finland tested subsea cable threat detection and Russia's satellite internet ambitions suffered a setback.

sections.heading: Nvidia Forges AI Infrastructure Partnerships Across South Korea
sections.body:
During a high-profile visit to Seoul, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang announced a series of strategic partnerships that substantially deepen the company's integration with Korea's technology ecosystem. SK Telecom committed to building a gigawatt-scale AI Cloud using Nvidia's DSX platform, with the first AI factory scheduled to come online in 2027. The initiative positions Korea's largest telecom operator to provide sovereign, physical, and agentic AI services for enterprises across the country.

Separately, SK hynix entered a multiyear technology partnership with Nvidia to co-develop next-generation memory products for AI factories, including components for Nvidia's Vera Rubin AI supercomputers. LG Group also announced collaborations spanning physical AI, robotics, autonomous driving, and AI infrastructure, with subsidiaries including LG Electronics, LG Energy Solution, and LG AI Research participating.

The partnerships reflect Korea's emergence as what Huang called 'extraordinary at manufacturing, mechatronics, and AI'—a combination that makes robotics and physical AI a strategic growth sector. SK Group Chairman Chey Tae-won framed the collaboration as addressing 'GPU, memory and energy challenges' while positioning Korea as 'a leading AI factory player shaping Asia's AI ecosystem.'
sections.whyItMatters: Nvidia is moving beyond chip sales to building sovereign AI infrastructure capacity in key allied nations. The SK Telecom gigawatt-scale AI Cloud signals that telecom operators are becoming national AI infrastructure providers—a shift with implications for compute sovereignty, energy policy, and how countries manage AI capabilities that governments increasingly treat as strategic assets.

sections.heading: Finland Tests Early Warning System for Subsea Cable Threats
sections.body:
Finland is testing an early warning system designed to detect threats to undersea telecommunications cables in the Baltic Sea, according to reporting by Japan Times. The initiative comes after multiple incidents of damaged cables, power connections, and gas pipelines in the region, which have raised concerns about infrastructure vulnerability and potential sabotage.

The Baltic Sea has seen several unexplained infrastructure incidents in recent years, prompting NATO and regional governments to increase surveillance and develop response capabilities. Finland's testing represents one of the first systematic efforts to build detection technology specifically for subsea cable threats.

The timing coincides with a separate US FCC proposal to tighten oversight of submarine communications cables that handle approximately 99% of international internet traffic. The proposed rules would make it harder for Chinese companies to provide equipment while creating fast-track approvals for trusted US technology firms.
sections.whyItMatters: Undersea cables carry the vast majority of global data traffic, yet remain vulnerable to both accidental damage and deliberate interference. Finland's initiative and the FCC's regulatory push signal that governments are treating subsea infrastructure as critical national security assets, with implications for equipment suppliers, route planning, and naval operations.

sections.heading: Russia's Starlink Alternative Suffers Early Setback
sections.body:
Russia's effort to build a domestic alternative to Starlink encountered difficulties when its first satellite lasted only 75 days before reentering the atmosphere, according to reports. The satellite reportedly never conducted a single orbital maneuver before its premature deorbit.

The Rassvet satellite internet network is expected to begin commercial service in 2027, according to TechRepublic reporting, as Moscow attempts to build sovereign communications capacity independent of Western providers. The project reflects Russia's broader push for technological self-sufficiency amid sanctions and export controls.

The failure highlights the technical challenges of building satellite constellations—an area where SpaceX's Starlink has accumulated significant operational experience. Russia's ambitions in this space remain contingent on successful orbital operations.
sections.whyItMatters: Satellite internet constellations have become dual-use infrastructure, supporting both civilian communications and military operations. Ukraine's successful use of Starlink demonstrated the strategic value of resilient satellite connectivity. Russia's parallel effort, if successful, would reduce dependence on foreign infrastructure—though the early failure suggests the gap between ambition and operational capability remains wide.

sections.heading: AI-Enabled Cyber Threats Growing More Sophisticated
sections.body:
A new report from Anthropic analyzing 832 AI-enabled cyberthreat cases from March 2025 to March 2026 found that malicious actors are increasingly using AI for attack preparation, including writing malware and disrupting system performance. In 67% of cases examined, threat actors used AI for attack preparation stages.

More concerning, the study found that 6.5% of attackers used AI to assist with 'lateral movement'—navigating deep inside compromised networks. The sophistication level rose significantly: in the first six months of research, 33% of attackers were rated medium-risk or higher; over the next six months, that figure rose to 56%, an approximately 70% increase.

Verizon's 2026 Data Breach Report notes that mobile devices are now the favored target for AI-driven cyberattacks, with 31% of breaches starting with software vulnerabilities and nearly 50% involving ransomware.
sections.whyItMatters: AI is democratizing advanced cyber capabilities, enabling less-skilled threat actors to conduct more complex operations. The rise in AI-assisted lateral movement—previously a hallmark of sophisticated state-sponsored actors—suggests the barrier to advanced persistent threat techniques is lowering. Mobile devices as primary targets also reflects the shift in enterprise attack surface toward endpoints.

sections.heading: Lithium Prices Rebound on Supply Disruption Concerns
sections.body:
Lithium prices have staged a sharp recovery in 2026, with CME lithium hydroxide contracts climbing approximately 86% since the start of the year, pushing prices above $20,000 per metric ton for the first time since late 2023. The immediate trigger was a disruption at CATL's Jianxiawo mine in China, where operations were suspended after the mine's license expired.

The rally highlights ongoing vulnerabilities in battery metal supply chains. Despite years of investment in new production capacity, global lithium supply remains sensitive to disruptions at key assets. The market response to a single mine suspension demonstrates how futures trading amplifies price movements beyond immediate physical impacts.

Indonesia continues positioning itself as a critical minerals hub, with officials emphasizing the country's role as a major producer of bauxite, copper, and nickel essential for clean energy systems and electric vehicles. The country's downstreaming policy, including bans on raw exports of nickel and bauxite, aims to transform Indonesia from raw material supplier into an industrial processing hub.
sections.whyItMatters: The lithium rebound underscores that despite efforts to diversify supply chains, critical mineral markets remain vulnerable to single-point failures. Indonesia's downstreaming push and China's dominance in processing mean that battery supply chain resilience depends on both mining capacity and the political choices of resource-holding nations.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Wartime Connectivity Resilience Offers Lessons for Europe
sections.body:
Ukraine has maintained approximately 90% telecommunications network uptime despite sustained Russian attacks on energy infrastructure, according to analysis by Tech.eu. The resilience stems from a decentralized network structure—many smaller local internet providers rather than dominance by a few major players—and regulatory requirements that operators maintain 72-hour backup power capabilities.

Mobile operators must equip 25% of their networks with generators for 72+ hour operation during outages, with the remainder supported by backup batteries. Kyivstar became the first European operator to launch Starlink's direct-to-cell satellite technology, with over 3 million subscribers registering for satellite SMS by January 2026.

Ukraine's telecom sector even increased revenues during the war, reaching €3.58 billion in 2025 with capital investment up 35% year-over-year. The sector attracted foreign investment, including Xavier Niel's acquisition of lifecell and Kyivstar's Nasdaq listing—signals of long-term confidence.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine has become a real-world laboratory for telecom resilience under sustained infrastructure attack. The combination of regulatory mandates for backup power, decentralized provider networks, and satellite integration offers a template that European nations are now studying as they develop frameworks like the Digital Networks Act for critical infrastructure protection.

sections.heading: Türkiye's Defense Industry Expands Export Ambitions
sections.body:
Türkiye's defense sector has emerged as a major exporter after two decades of state investment, transforming from reliance on foreign arms makers to supplying nearly 40 countries, primarily in the Gulf, Africa, and Asia. Following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, European countries are reassessing security dependencies and turning to Türkiye as an industrial partner.

According to Devdiscourse analysis, Türkiye aims to double defense exports as global military spending rises and Western suppliers face procurement bottlenecks. The country's ability to swiftly enter markets where Western procurement cycles create delays has become a competitive advantage.

The sector's growth reflects broader patterns in defense industrialization—nations building domestic capacity to reduce import dependence and gain strategic flexibility in foreign policy.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye's defense export expansion illustrates how regional powers are building indigenous defense capacity that creates new supply chain options for third countries. The ability to bypass Western procurement bottlenecks gives Türkiye leverage in relationships with Gulf and African states, while providing alternative sources for European rearmament efforts.

bottomLine: Nvidia's Seoul partnerships mark a significant expansion of sovereign AI infrastructure building, with SK Telecom's gigawatt-scale AI Cloud representing the kind of national-level compute capacity that governments increasingly treat as strategic infrastructure. Meanwhile, Finland's subsea cable detection tests and Ukraine's 90% telecom uptime under fire both point to a growing focus on infrastructure resilience. Russia's satellite internet failure shows that building alternatives to Western technology remains technically challenging—even for a major power. The lithium rebound reminds us that critical mineral supply chains remain vulnerable to single-point disruptions, while Türkiye's defense export ambitions demonstrate how regional powers are carving out industrial niches in the global rearmament push.
sources: UPI, Manila Times/GlobeNewswire, Reuters, Tech.eu, Anthropic, China Daily, Devdiscourse, Japan Times, Bloomberg, TechRepublic, MSN, IBTimes, Verizon Data Breach Report
